ground_truth.title: Yoga: Methods, types, philosophy, and risks
ground_truth.author: Hannah Nichols
ground_truth.main_content: Yoga is a mind and body practice that combines movement, meditation, and breathing. People practice yoga for many reasons, from physical fitness to spiritual enlightenment. This article explores the history, philosophy, health and wellness benefits, and various branches of yoga. The history of yoga spans 2,500 to 5,000 years, with the first mention of "yoga" appearing in one of the oldest collections of writing in the world: the Rigveda. Yoga combines three key elements: movements or postures (asana), breathing (pranayama), and meditation (dhyana). Later, the sage Patanjali taught that yoga has eight "limbs": outward conduct (yama), personal conduct (niyama), physical postures (asana), breath work (pranayama), withdrawal of the senses (pratyahara), concentration (dharana), meditation (dhyana), and enlightenment (samadhi). Another aspect of yoga is the belief in chakras, which are centers of energy within the human body. There are seven major chakras in Hinduism: Sahasrara (crown), Ajna (third eye), Vishuddha (throat), Anahata (heart), Manipura (solar plexus), Svadhishthana (sacral), and Muladhara (root). Types of yoga include Hatha, Ashtanga, Bikram (hot yoga), Iyengar, Kripalu, Kundalini, Sivananda, Yin, and Restorative. Research suggests yoga has many benefits including reducing stress, improving sleep, improving balance and flexibility, and reducing muscle tension, headaches, neck pain, and back pain. Many types of yoga are safe when a well-trained instructor is guiding the practice, though there is a risk of sprains and strains. Beginners should slowly build up to more challenging poses.
with: Yoga is a mind-body practice that combines movements, or postures (asana), breathing (pranayama), and meditation (dhyana).
The history of yoga spans 2,500 to 5,000 years, with the first mention of "yoga" appearing in one of the oldest collections of writing in the world: the Rigveda.
The sage Patanjali taught that yoga has eight "limbs": yama, niyama, asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dharana, dhyana, and samadhi.
Research suggests that yoga has many physical and mental benefits, including reducing stress, improving sleep, and improving balance and flexibility.
Many types of yoga are safe when a well-trained instructor is guiding the practice, though the most common injuries are sprains and strains.
